content:
# Matthew Chapter 11 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_011_page_001.jpg, MAT_011_page_002.jpg

### Jesus and John the Baptist

1. After Jesus finished giving instructions to his twelve disciples, he moved on to teach and preach in the surrounding towns.

2. When John, who was in prison, heard about what the Messiah was doing, he sent his disciples to ask him,

3. "Are you really the one we've been waiting for, or should we expect someone else?"

4. Jesus replied, "Go back and report to John what you're hearing and seeing:

5. Blind people are seeing, paralyzed people are walking, lepers are being healed, deaf people are hearing, dead people are being raised to life, and the poor are hearing good news.

6. And blessed is the person who doesn't stumble over me."

7. As John's disciples were leaving, Jesus began speaking to the crowd about John: "What did you go out into the wilderness to see? A reed blowing in the wind?

8. What did you go out to see? A man in expensive clothes? People who wear expensive clothes live in palaces.

9. So what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes — and I tell you, far more than a prophet.

10. This is the one the Scripture speaks about: 'Look, I am sending my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way before you.'

11. I tell you the truth: among everyone ever born, no one is greater than John the Baptist. Yet even the least important person in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he is.

12. From the time John the Baptist began preaching until now, the kingdom of heaven has been under assault, and violent people are trying to seize it.

13. For all the Prophets and the Law pointed forward until John arrived.

14. And if you're willing to accept it, he is the Elijah who was predicted to come.

15. Anyone with ears to hear should listen and understand!

### Jesus Criticizes This Generation

16. What can I compare this generation to? They're like children sitting in the marketplace, calling out to each other:

17. 'We played the flute and you didn't dance! We sang funeral songs and you didn't cry!'

18. John came fasting and living simply, and they said, 'He's possessed by a demon.'

19. The Son of Man came feasting and enjoying life, and they say, 'Look at him — a glutton and a drunk, hanging out with tax collectors and sinners!' But wisdom proves herself right by her actions."

### Woe to Unrepentant Towns

20. Then Jesus began to condemn the towns where he had performed most of his miracles, because they refused to turn from their sins.

21. "How terrible for you, Chorazin! How terrible for you, Bethsaida! If the miracles done among you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, those pagan cities would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.

22. I tell you, Tyre and Sidon will be better off on judgment day than you.

23. And you, Capernaum — do you think you'll be lifted up to heaven? You'll be thrown down to the grave. If the miracles done among you had been done in Sodom, it would still be standing today.

24. I tell you, even Sodom will be better off on judgment day than you."

### Rest for the Weary

25. At that time Jesus prayed, "I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the intellectuals and experts and revealed them to those who are childlike.

26. Yes, Father — this is exactly what pleased you.

27. My Father has entrusted everything to me. No one truly knows the Son except the Father, and no one truly knows the Father except the Son — and those the Son chooses to reveal him to.

28. Come to me, all of you who are exhausted and weighed down, and I will give you rest.

29. Walk with me and learn from me, because I am gentle and humble at heart — and you will find rest for your souls.

30. My yoke is easy to bear, and my burden is light."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 5 — The messianic checklist:** Jesus doesn't answer John's question directly. Instead, he points to what's happening: the blind see, the lame walk, the dead rise, the poor hear good news. This is a deliberate echo of Isaiah 35:5-6 and 61:1 — the signs that the age of salvation has begun. The evidence is the answer.

**Verse 12 — "under assault":** This is one of the most debated verses in the Gospels. The Greek βιάζεται can mean "is forcefully advancing" (positive) or "is suffering violence" (negative). In context — John imprisoned, Jesus rejected — the violent opposition reading fits best. The kingdom is breaking in, and it's meeting fierce resistance.

**Verse 19 — "wisdom proves herself right by her actions":** The Sinaiticus reads "works/deeds" (ἔργων), not "children." Wisdom is personified — she is vindicated not by argument but by results. Both John's austere path and Jesus' celebratory path are wisdom's work.

**Verses 28-30 — "Come to me":** This is Jesus' most tender invitation. The "yoke" is a rabbinic term for a teacher's interpretation of the Torah — every rabbi placed a "yoke" on his students. The Pharisees' yoke was notoriously heavy (see 23:4). Jesus offers a different kind: gentle, humble, light. Not lawlessness — but a different way of carrying the load.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
